summary:
The Kingdom of Jerusalem (1099–1291 AD) was the most important of the Crusader states established following the [[event:2220|First Crusade]], a Latin Christian kingdom centered on the holy city of Jerusalem that represented the primary objective of the crusading movement and whose defense or recovery motivated subsequent crusades for nearly two centuries. Founded following the [[event:2221|capture of Jerusalem]] in 1099 with Godfrey of Bouillon as its first ruler (as 'Advocate of the Holy Sepulchre'), the kingdom at its greatest extent controlled the coastal strip of modern Israel, the West Bank, and Jordan, plus nominal suzerainty over the other Crusader states of Antioch, Tripoli, and Edessa.

The Kingdom of Jerusalem was a unique political entity — a European feudal monarchy transplanted to the Levant, maintaining a French-speaking ruling class who gradually adapted to their environment, trading extensively with Muslim neighbors even during nominal states of war, and developing a distinctive synthesis of European, Byzantine, and Levantine culture. The kingdom's loss of Jerusalem to [[event:2273|Saladin]] at the [[event:2274|Battle of Hattin]] (1187) and the city's recapture reduced the kingdom to its coastal cities, which survived under constant military pressure for another century with ongoing Crusader reinforcements. The kingdom was formally extinguished when the last Crusader stronghold [[event:3531|Acre fell]] in 1291. In its two centuries of existence, the Kingdom of Jerusalem represented Europe's most sustained attempt at colonial settlement in the medieval Middle East, leaving architectural monuments (the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in its current form, Crusader castles) and cultural legacies that shaped the medieval Islamic and Christian worlds.
